title: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model
hook: What if you could align a language model without any separate reward model?
promise: You will understand how DPO lets language models learn human preferences directly, skipping a separate reward model.
prerequisite_note: You only need to know that language models can be tuned to prefer certain responses over others.

glossary.plain: The process of training models to prefer outputs that humans like more.
A model that assigns scores to outputs, reflecting how much humans might prefer them.
A method that lets the language model itself learn preferences, without needing a separate reward model.
A measure of how different two probability distributions are from each other.
A training setup where the model is shown two outputs and must learn which one is better.
A method where models are improved using human feedback and reinforcement learning.

cards.title: Train Preferences Directly
body: Discover how DPO lets language models learn human preferences without a reward model.

cards.title: Why does aligning models matter?
body: Imagine you want your AI assistant to answer kindly and helpfully, not just correctly. How do you teach it what people actually prefer?
Previous approaches used a separate reward model to score answers, but this extra step made training slow and complex. DPO asks: can we remove this step entirely?

cards.title: From Preferences to Model Updates
body: DPO starts with pairs of outputs, where one is preferred over the other by humans. The model observes these choices directly.
Instead of learning a reward model, DPO updates the language model so it assigns higher probability to preferred answers. It uses math to ensure the model shifts just enough.
The outcome is a language model that acts as its own reward model, learning to generate preferred responses without extra machinery.
cards.caption: DPO cuts out the reward model, letting the language model learn preferences in one step.

cards.title: Reward Model or Direct Preference?
body: Toggle to see how training changes with or without a separate reward model.
cards.offNote: Needs to train a reward model, then use RL to tune the language model. Slow and complex.
cards.onNote: No reward model needed. The language model learns preferences directly, making training simpler and faster.

cards.title: The DPO Trick
body: DPO realizes that the language model can be trained to prefer one response over another by adjusting its probabilities directly, using only which answer humans liked.
It uses a clever mathematical formula to nudge the model toward preferred answers, without ever building a separate reward scoring system.

cards.title: How much should the model shift?
body: Move the slider to see how aggressively the model can be tuned toward preferred responses.
cards.low: Model barely changes, stays close to its original behavior.
cards.mid: Model leans toward preferences but remains cautious.
cards.high: Model strongly favors preferred responses, risking overfitting to preferences.

cards.title: What does DPO actually need?
body: Select the ingredients needed for DPO training.

choices.label: Human Preference Pairs
choices.note: Pairs of outputs, one preferred by humans.

choices.label: Pairwise Loss Function
choices.note: Mathematical rule to compare preferred and non-preferred outputs.

choices.label: KL Regularization
choices.note: Keeps the model from drifting too far from its original self.

choices.label: Optimizer
choices.note: Adjusts the model's parameters to improve preference alignment.

choices.label: Scaling Factor
choices.note: Controls how strongly to push the model toward preferences.

cards.title: DPO: Simpler, Faster, Better?
body: DPO matched or beat previous methods like RLHF on preference alignment tasks, with less engineering and training time.
It produced models that humans preferred, while using fewer resources and avoiding the instability of reinforcement learning.

bars.label: Preference Win Rate
bars.note: How often DPO's outputs are preferred

cards.caption: DPO achieved strong results with a simpler and more stable approach.

cards.title: A Canonical Shift in Alignment
body: DPO changed how we think about aligning language models, showing that the model itself can learn preferences without a separate reward step.
This simplification could make future models easier to align, opening the door to safer and more efficient AI.

recap.label: Compare
recap.note: Use pairs to see which outputs are preferred.

recap.label: Update
recap.note: Adjust model probabilities toward preferred outputs.

recap.label: Align
recap.note: Model generates responses that match human preferences.

cards.title: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model
body: Read the original paper for the technical details and mathematical proofs behind DPO.
